title: The Passive Form — Being Acted Upon

meta.focus: Express that the subject receives an action using the passive voice. Covers the direct passive (agent marked with に), the adversative/suffering passive, and the potential vs passive ambiguity in RU-verbs.

sections.title: The Passive Form — Being Acted Upon
sections.summary: The passive voice flips the focus of a sentence: instead of 'A does X to B', the sentence becomes 'B is X-ed by A'. Japanese uses a passive form suffix — 〜られる for RU-verbs and 〜れる for U-verbs — to express that the subject receives an action. The passive has two main uses: the neutral direct passive (reporting who received what) and the adversative or suffering passive (expressing that something unwanted happened to the speaker or someone else).
sections.whyItMatters: The passive form appears everywhere — in news reports, formal writing, daily conversation, and storytelling. You will need it to describe things done to you, to avoid naming the agent of an action (which is more polite in Japanese than in English), and to express the frustration of 'someone did something to me'. Without the passive, large areas of natural Japanese are simply unreachable.
youWillLearn: How to form the passive for RU-verbs, U-verbs, する, and くる
The direct passive — subject receives the action, agent marked with に (先生に 聞かれた = I was asked by the teacher)
The adversative/suffering passive — expressing that something unwanted happened to you (友だちに 答えを 教えられた = My friend told me the answer [and I suffered for it])
When to drop the agent — passive sentences without に are natural when the agent is obvious or unimportant
Passive vs potential ambiguity for RU-verbs — 食べられる means both 'can eat' and 'is eaten', and context resolves it

sections.title: Passive Form — Conjugation Chart
sections.description: The passive suffix attaches differently depending on verb type. RU-verbs (ichidan) drop る and add られる. U-verbs (godan) shift the final mora to the あ-row and add れる. Note: する and くる are irregular.

rows.label: RU-verb (ichidan)
rows.meaning: is eaten / can eat (ambiguous for RU-verbs — context resolves)

notes: U-verb あ-row shift: く→か、ぐ→が、す→さ、つ→た、ぬ→な、ぶ→ば、む→ま、る→ら. Exception: う→わ (not うあ).
The passive form ends in a RU-verb suffix (〜られる / 〜れる), so it conjugates further like a RU-verb in all other forms (て-form, polite, past, etc.).
For RU-verbs, the passive and potential forms look identical (食べられる). Context is the only disambiguator — see the third grammar rule below.

sections.explanation: In the direct passive, the person or thing that RECEIVES the action becomes the grammatical subject (marked by は or が). The person who PERFORMS the action — the agent — is marked with に. This に is different from the destination に — here it marks the source of the action. The passive form itself is conjugated to match the tense and politeness level of the sentence.
notes: Pattern: [receiver]は [agent]に [verb-passive]. Example: りきぞは 先生に 聞かれた。(Rikizo was asked by the teacher.)
The agent (に) can be dropped when the agent is unknown, unimportant, or obvious from context: この 本は 多くの 人に 読まれた。(This book was read by many people — or simply: この 本は 読まれた。)
Objects: the original direct object can remain in the sentence with を. Example: りきぞは 先生に 名前を 聞かれた。(Rikizo was asked his name by the teacher.)
Politeness: passive forms conjugate like RU-verbs. Polite past: 〜られました / 〜れました.

In written and formal Japanese, the passive often replaces active sentences as a stylistic preference — especially in news, explanations, and descriptions where the agent is less important than the result.

parts.gloss: Rikizo (は — topic / receiver)
by the teacher (に — agent marker)
name (を — object; the thing asked about)
was asked (聞く → 聞かれる — U-verb passive, polite past)

examples.en: Rikizo was asked his name by the teacher.
examples.breakdown: 聞く is a U-verb (く-ending). Passive: く → か + れる = 聞かれる. Polite past: 聞かれました. The agent (先生) is marked with に, not で or から.

parts.gloss: this book (は — topic / receiver)
by many people (に — agent)
is being read (読む → 読まれる — U-verb passive, progressive)

examples.en: This book is being read by many people.
examples.breakdown: 読む is a U-verb (む-ending). Passive: む → ま + れる = 読まれる. Progressive passive: 読まれて + います. The agent phrase 多くの人に can be dropped if the reading is the focus.

sections.meaning: Someone suffered because [agent] did X to them
sections.explanation: The adversative passive (迷惑の受け身) expresses that something unwanted or inconvenient happened to the subject. Unlike the direct passive, the subject here is affected by — but is NOT the direct object of — the original action. The subject simply suffered because someone else acted. Japanese uses the passive form to encode this sense of being negatively impacted by another's action. This pattern is common in complaints, sympathy, and storytelling about difficulties.
notes: The key signal: the subject (は) is HARMED or INCONVENIENCED by what the に-agent did.
The original action may have its own object: 友だちに 問題の 答えを 教えられた。(My friend told me the answer to the problem — adversative: being told the answer was unwanted.)

Intransitive adversative passive: the subject doesn't need to be the object at all — the person just suffered the consequence. 友だちに 話されて、よくなかった。(My friend kept talking [on and on] and it wasn't good.)
The adversative passive carries a tone of inconvenience, frustration, or victimhood. Context determines whether the passive is neutral (direct) or adversative — the form is identical.
The passive is especially used in storytelling to express what happened TO the speaker without the speaker's consent or desire.

parts.gloss: Rikizo (は — topic / sufferer)
by his friend (に — agent of the action)
the answer to the problem (の — possessive, を — object)
was told / was taught (教える → 教えられる — RU-verb passive, plain past)

examples.en: His friend told Rikizo the answer to the problem. (Rikizo suffered from being told — he didn't want to know.)
examples.breakdown: 教える is a RU-verb (ichidan). Passive: drop る, add られる = 教えられる. Plain past: 教えられた. The adversative nuance: Rikizo didn't want to be given the answer — being told it was an unwanted action.

parts.gloss: Rikizo (は — topic / sufferer)
by the teacher (に — agent)
in Japanese (で — means / method)
was spoken to (話す → 話される, te-form — adversative)

parts.text: あまり わかりませんでした。
parts.gloss: didn't understand much (あまり + negative — polite past)

examples.en: The teacher spoke to Rikizo in Japanese, and he didn't understand much. (He suffered from being spoken to in Japanese.)
examples.breakdown: 話す → 話される (U-verb passive: す → さ + れる). Te-form: 話されて. The te-form links the unwanted action (being spoken to) to its consequence (not understanding). This double-predicate structure is a hallmark of the adversative passive.

sections.meaning: RU-verb + られる = passive OR potential — only context decides
sections.explanation: For RU-verbs (ichidan verbs), the passive form 〜られる is identical to the potential form 〜られる. Japanese speakers use context — including the presence or absence of an agent に, the nature of the subject, and the surrounding conversation — to understand which meaning is intended. This is not an error in the language; native speakers navigate it unconsciously. U-verbs have no ambiguity: 話される (passive) vs 話せる (potential) are distinct.
notes: 食べられる: passive = 'is eaten' (e.g. 虫に 食べられる = eaten by insects); potential = 'can eat' (e.g. 辛いものが 食べられる = can eat spicy food).
Agent に disambiguates toward passive: [person]に 食べられる → passive (eaten by someone). No agent + が/は subject → potential (can eat).

Spoken casual Japanese often shortens the potential to 〜れる (食べれる, 見れる) — called ら抜き言葉 (ra-nuki kotoba). This shortens only the POTENTIAL; the passive always keeps られる. So: 見られる = passive or potential (polite/standard); 見れる = potential only (casual).
In ambiguous contexts, speakers use ことができる for potential to avoid confusion: 食べることができる (can eat) is unambiguous.
For this lesson, focus on reading context to determine meaning. Producing passive sentences is the main skill; passive vs potential recognition is secondary.

parts.gloss: this book (は — topic)
in one day (で — time frame)
is read (読む → 読まれる — U-verb, unambiguously passive)

examples.en: This book is read in one day. (passive — U-verb 読む, so no ambiguity with potential)
examples.breakdown: 読む is a U-verb: 読まれる is ONLY passive. 読める is the potential. U-verbs never create ambiguity — only RU-verbs do.

parts.gloss: Rikizo (は — topic)
by the teacher (に — agent marker → passive signal)
was seen / watched (見る → 見られた — passive, plain past)

examples.en: Rikizo was watched by the teacher. (passive reading — agent に present, human subject being acted on)
examples.breakdown: 先生に (agent に) strongly signals passive. Rikizo is the one being watched, not the one doing the watching. Without 先生に, 見られる would be ambiguous — but the agent に removes all doubt.

sections.title: The Passive in Context — News Style and Daily Life

examples.context: News-style passive — agent omitted, focus on the result

parts.gloss: new school (が — subject / receiver)
in the town (に — location)
was built (作る → 作られる — U-verb passive, polite past)

examples.en: A new school was built in the town.
examples.note: No agent (に) needed — the builder is unimportant or unknown. This agent-less passive is common in announcements and news. 作る → 作られる: る-ending godan verb (not ichidan), passive: る → ら + れる = 作られる.

examples.context: Direct passive — agent named with に

parts.gloss: Rikizo (は — topic / receiver)
by the teacher (に — agent)
a Japanese-language problem (の — modifier, を — object)
was asked (聞く → 聞かれる — U-verb passive, polite past)

examples.en: Rikizo was asked a Japanese-language problem by the teacher.
examples.note: 聞く → 聞かれる: く → か + れる. The object (問題を) stays in the passive sentence — Rikizo received the action of being asked about the problem.

parts.gloss: Rikizo (は — topic / sufferer)
by his friend (に — agent)
the answer (を — object of what was told)
was told [it] (教える → 教えられる, te-form — adversative; he suffered)
it wasn't good (よい → よくない — plain negative, plain past adj)

examples.en: His friend told Rikizo the answer, and it wasn't good. (He suffered from being told the answer.)
examples.note: 教えられて → よくなかった: the te-form of the passive links the unwanted action (being told) to its consequence. This double-predicate structure is a hallmark of the adversative passive in storytelling.

examples.context: Passive vs potential — context resolves 見られる

parts.gloss: Rikizo (は — topic)
every day by the teacher (に — agent → passive signal)
is watched (見る → 見られる, plain passive) + と (quotation)
thinks / feels (と思っている — sustained belief, polite)

examples.en: Rikizo feels he is being watched by the teacher every day.
examples.note: 毎日 先生に → passive reading is clear. The presence of an agent (先生に) rules out potential. と思っています reinforces the G27 sustained-belief pattern. Without 先生に, 見られる could mean 'can see' or 'is seen' — the agent makes all the difference.

sections.title: きのう のこと (Casual)
sections.context: りきぞ and けん are close friends chatting at school about things that happened to them. Casual register.

lines.jp: りきぞ、きのう 先生に 日本語で 話されたけど、分かった？
lines.en: Rikizo, the teacher spoke to you in Japanese yesterday — did you understand?

lines.jp: うん、ちょっと むずかしかったけど、分かった。先生に 問題も 聞かれたよ。
lines.en: Yeah, it was a bit difficult, but I understood. I was also asked a problem by the teacher.

lines.jp: そう なんだ。私も きのう 友だちに 日本語で 話されたから、あまり 分からなかった。
lines.en: Oh really. Because I was also spoken to in Japanese by a friend yesterday, I didn't understand much.

lines.jp: どうして？ むずかしかった から？
うん。でも、先生に 注意 されたから、もっと 勉強 しようと 思った。
lines.en: Yeah. But because I was cautioned by the teacher, I thought I should study more.

lines.jp: いいね。毎日 勉強 すれば、もっと 分かるように なると 思うよ。
lines.en: That's good. I think if you study every day, you'll come to understand more.

sections.title: Build the Passive Form
sections.instructions: Change each verb from its dictionary form into the passive form. Pay attention to whether it is a RU-verb or a U-verb.

items.hint: Drop る, add られる
Drop る, add られる, then plain past
く → か + れる → polite
する is irregular: passive = される
Drop る, add られる, then polite past

sections.title: Practice: The Passive Form
sections.instructions: Choose the best answer for each question.

items.q: Which sentence correctly means 'I was asked (a question) by the teacher'?
choices: 先生に 聞かれました。
先生を 聞きました。
先生が 聞かれました。
先生に 聞きました。
items.answer: 先生に 聞かれました。
items.explanation: 先生に (agent に) + 聞かれました (passive of 聞く, polite past). The agent of the action (the teacher) is marked with に in a passive sentence. 先生に聞きました = I asked the teacher (active). 先生を聞きました is ungrammatical. 先生が聞かれました = the teacher was asked (makes the teacher the receiver, not Rikizo).

items.q: りきぞは 友だちに 答えを 教えられた。What does this sentence mean?
choices: Rikizo's friend told Rikizo the answer (and Rikizo suffered from it).
Rikizo taught the answer to his friend.
Rikizo wanted to teach his friend the answer.
Rikizo's friend was taught the answer by Rikizo.
items.answer: Rikizo's friend told Rikizo the answer (and Rikizo suffered from it).
items.explanation: りきぞは (receiver / topic) + 友だちに (agent — his friend) + 答えを (object — the answer) + 教えられた (passive past of 教える = was taught/told). The passive here is adversative: りきぞ received an action he likely didn't want. 教える → 教えられる (RU-verb passive: drop る, add られる).

items.q: 食べられる can mean 'can eat' OR 'is eaten'. Which context makes it passive?
choices: この ケーキは 友だちに 食べられた。(This cake was eaten by a friend.)
私は この 魚が 食べられる。(I can eat this fish.)
子どもは それが 食べられない。(Children can't eat that.)
りきぞは 朝ご飯を 食べられる。(Rikizo can eat breakfast.)
items.answer: この ケーキは 友だちに 食べられた。(This cake was eaten by a friend.)
items.explanation: 友だちに (agent に) + 食べられた = passive reading: the cake was eaten BY a friend. The presence of an agent (に) is the strongest signal that 食べられた is passive, not potential. All other choices show the potential meaning — they have no agent に, and the subject is the one doing the eating (expressing ability).

items.q: 話す is a U-verb ending in す. What is its passive form?
items.explanation: U-verb す-ending passive rule: す → さ + れる = 話される. 話させる = causative (make/let someone speak). 話せる = potential (can speak). 話せられる is not a real form. The passive always uses the あ-row shift: す → さ.

items.q: Which sentence uses the adversative (suffering) passive most naturally?
choices: 私は 友だちに 問題の 答えを 教えられて、よくなかった。
この 本は 多くの 人に 読まれています。
新しい 学校が 町に 作られました。
その 話は 先生に 聞かれました。
items.answer: 私は 友だちに 問題の 答えを 教えられて、よくなかった。
items.explanation: The adversative passive expresses that the speaker suffered from an unwanted action. わたしは + 友だちに (agent) + 教えられて + よくなかった = the friend told the speaker something unwanted, with a negative consequence. The other choices are neutral direct passives: the book is read by many people, a school was built, a story was heard — none of these imply suffering by the subject.

items.q: けんは 先生に 注意 されました。What was the agent of this action?
choices: The sentence doesn't name the agent
items.explanation: In a passive sentence, the agent (the one doing the action) is marked with に. 先生に = 'by the teacher'. けんは is the receiver (topic) — Ken received the warning. 注意 is the noun part of the suru-compound 注意する (to caution / to warn). されました is the polite past passive of する.
